intro: Python provides cross-platform agility through structural dynamic typing and runtime flexibility. Transitioning to Swift delivers compiled performance, static guarantees, and native optimization targeting Apple platforms and performance-critical server backends. This migration path is selected when developers need to eliminate runtime errors, build native consumer applications, or optimize compute efficiency.

diffs.from: Dynamic, strongly typed with type hinting capabilities.
diffs.to: Static, strongly typed with rigorous type inference.

diffs.from: Tracing Garbage Collection and reference counting.
diffs.to: Deterministic Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) enforced at compile time.

diffs.from: None checking handled entirely at runtime.
diffs.to: Compile-time Optional types preventing unexpected null references.

diffs.from: Objects are reference-passed; simple primitives behave like values.
diffs.to: Explicit Value types (structs, enums) vs Reference types (classes).

diffs.from: Interpreted bytecode with significant runtime overhead.
diffs.to: Compiled directly to LLVM machine instructions for raw performance.

examples.title: Optional Handling
examples.description: Safely unwrapping a variable that may containing a value or null.

examples.title: Struct Definitions
examples.description: Creating lightweight, immutable data structures with methods.

tips.title: Break Reference Cycles
tips.body: Declare delegate or block references as "weak" or "unowned" to prevent memory leaks in Swift's ARC engine.

tips.title: Default to Structs
tips.body: Prefer structs over classes by default to capitalize on value semantics and thread-safe data copying.

tips.title: Embrace Guard Statements
tips.body: Use early exits with "guard let" to unwrap optionals cleanly and minimize nested logic structures.

tips.title: Specify Mutability explicitly
tips.body: Use "let" for all immutable constant values and "var" only when values must actively change.
